age: Mean age 61 years (SD 19) overall; ICU mean 62 (SD 17); non-ICU mean 61 (SD 20)
lactate: Serum lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L in 4% overall; 21% in ICU-admitted
gender: Not reported as overall breakdown; male sex associated with OR 1.56 (95% CI 1.45-1.68) for mortality in derivation cohort
notes: 64% (95% CI, 62%-67%) of decedents had ≥2 SIRS criteria; 65% (95% CI, 64%-65%) of survivors had <2 SIRS criteria. Baseline model AUROC = 0.58 (95% CI, 0.57-0.60) in ICU and 0.69 (95% CI, 0.68-0.70) outside ICU.

population: Patients with infection, in ICU

notes: 70% (95% CI, 69%-72%) of decedents had ≥2 qSOFA points; 78% (95% CI, 78%-79%) of survivors had <2 qSOFA points

population: Encounters in KPNC data (suspected infection)
method: AUROC comparison (post hoc addition of serum lactate to qSOFA)
effect_size: AUROC with lactate = 0.80 (95% CI, 0.79-0.81) vs AUROC without lactate = 0.79 (95% CI, 0.78-0.80)
performance: P < .001; however, clinical relevance was small as rates of in-hospital mortality across deciles of risk were numerically similar with or without lactate
age: Mean age 61 years (SD 19) overall; ICU mean 62 (SD 17); non-ICU mean 61 (SD 20)
lactate: Serum lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L in 4% overall; 21% in ICU-admitted
gender: Not reported as overall breakdown; male sex associated with OR 1.56 (95% CI 1.45-1.68) for mortality in derivation cohort
notes: Serum lactate did not meet prespecified statistical thresholds for inclusion in qSOFA during model building in UPMC data. Post hoc analysis in KPNC data showed statistically significant but clinically small improvement. Consistent results for higher lactate thresholds (3.0 mmol/L, 4.0 mmol/L) and continuous distribution (P < .001). Encounters with 1 qSOFA point plus lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L had in-hospital mortality numerically similar to encounters with 2 qSOFA points without lactate.

source_info.section: Time Windows for Measuring qSOFA Variables

population: Encounters with suspected infection in the ICU and outside the ICU
outcome: Predictive validity for sepsis (life-threatening organ dysfunction due to dysregulated host response to infection)
standardized_outcome: Predictive validity for sepsis (life-threatening organ dysfunction due to dysregulated host response to infection)
method: Comparison of predictive validity across clinical criteria
performance: SOFA and LODS had statistically greater predictive validity compared with SIRS criteria in ICU encounters; qSOFA assessed outside ICU
age: Mean age 61 years (SD 19) overall; ICU mean 62 (SD 17); non-ICU mean 61 (SD 20)
lactate: Serum lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L in 4% overall; 21% in ICU-admitted
gender: Not reported as overall breakdown; male sex associated with OR 1.56 (95% CI 1.45-1.68) for mortality in derivation cohort
notes: Third International Consensus Definitions Task Force (Sepsis-3). No gold-standard test for sepsis; validity assessed across multiple domains. Full results truncated in excerpt.

population: Patients with suspected infection in ICU and non-ICU settings, academic and community hospitals, international locations
method: Comparison of predictive validity (ROC/AUC analysis implied)
performance: Statistically greater predictive validity than SOFA score in non-ICU settings; statistically inferior compared with SOFA for ICU encounters
age: Mean age 61 years (SD 19) overall; ICU mean 62 (SD 17); non-ICU mean 61 (SD 20)
lactate: Serum lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L in 4% overall; 21% in ICU-admitted
gender: Not reported as overall breakdown; male sex associated with OR 1.56 (95% CI 1.45-1.68) for mortality in derivation cohort
notes: Task force recommended SOFA score ≥2 points for sepsis criteria in encounters with infection; qSOFA recommended in non-ICU settings to identify possibility of sepsis. qSOFA has statistically lower content validity as a measure of multiorgan dysfunction compared with SOFA.

population: Infected patients outside of the ICU
method: Comparison of predictive validity between qSOFA and SOFA/LODS scores
performance: qSOFA predictive validity outside of the ICU statistically greater than SOFA score (P < .001)
age: Mean age 61 years (SD 19) overall; ICU mean 62 (SD 17); non-ICU mean 61 (SD 20)
lactate: Serum lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L in 4% overall; 21% in ICU-admitted
gender: Not reported as overall breakdown; male sex associated with OR 1.56 (95% CI 1.45-1.68) for mortality in derivation cohort
notes: qSOFA uses no laboratory tests; qSOFA and SOFA scores had acceptable agreement in the majority of encounters; qSOFA was derived and tested among patient encounters in which infection was already suspected; mental status assessed as abnormal vs. normal (not change from baseline)

population: Intermediate-risk encounters (qSOFA score = 1) in KPNC health system
method: Post hoc addition of serum lactate to qSOFA model
performance: Risk profile similar to those with 2 qSOFA points; statistically increased predictive validity with little difference in encounter classification
age: Mean age 61 years (SD 19) overall; ICU mean 62 (SD 17); non-ICU mean 61 (SD 20)
lactate: Serum lactate ≥2.0 mmol/L in 4% overall; 21% in ICU-admitted
gender: Not reported as overall breakdown; male sex associated with OR 1.56 (95% CI 1.45-1.68) for mortality in derivation cohort
notes: Serum lactate was not retained in qSOFA during original model construction, partly because it was not commonly measured in the UPMC data set. Analysis assessed lactate contribution above and beyond qSOFA model. Suggests lactate may substitute for individual qSOFA variables or be used for borderline qSOFA values in health systems with reliable low-cost lactate measurement.
